question_title: Will a daily global surface air temperature anomaly of at least +1.5°C above pre-industrial be recorded between June 1 and June 26, 2026 (per Copernicus Climate Pulse)?
summary: This question has effectively already resolved YES based on existing finalized data. The Copernicus Climate Pulse ERA5 daily CSV shows June 6 (+0.651°C), June 7 (+0.653°C), and June 8 (+0.646°C) all had 1991-2020 anomalies that, combined with the fixed +0.88°C pre-industrial offset, yield +1.526 to +1.533°C — exceeding the +1.50°C threshold. All three days carry 'Final' data status. The margins of +0.026 to +0.033°C above threshold are sufficient to survive minor revisions. The only residual risk (~3%) comes from unlikely scenarios: dashboard rendering discrepancies, unexpected ERA5 data corrections, or interpretation ambiguities — all of which are very small given the explicit resolution criteria and backup data provisions.

factors.description: Multiple days (June 6, 7, 8) already show FINAL ERA5 data with 1991-2020 anomalies of +0.646 to +0.653°C, yielding pre-industrial anomalies of +1.526 to +1.533°C — well above the +1.50°C threshold. Data retrieved from the exact CSV source underlying the Copernicus Climate Pulse dashboard.

Three independent days exceed the threshold with margins of +0.026 to +0.033°C. All are marked 'Final' status (not preliminary), making data revision very unlikely. Even if one day were revised down, two others would still exceed.

Small risk that Climate Pulse dashboard displays slightly different values than the underlying CSV on resolution day June 27, or that there's a dashboard outage. Fine print allows ERA5-based data as backup, mitigating this risk.
Negligible risk of offset interpretation issues — resolution criteria explicitly state the fixed +0.88°C offset regardless of any C3S revisions.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.97 (97% YES)**

## Key Findings

The question has effectively **already resolved YES** based on existing finalized data:

| Date | 1991-2020 Anomaly | Pre-Industrial Anomaly | Margin Above +1.5°C | Status |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------------|--------|
| June 6 | +0.651°C | +1.531°C | +0.031°C | **Final** |
| June 7 | +0.653°C | +1.533°C | +0.033°C | **Final** |
| June 8 | +0.646°C | +1.526°C | +0.026°C | **Final** |
| June 11 | +0.622°C | +1.502°C | +0.002°C | **Final** |

**Three independent days** (June 6-8) clearly exceed the +1.50°C pre-industrial threshold with finalized ERA5 data, using the exact CSV source underlying the Climate Pulse dashboard. The ~3% discount from certainty accounts for unlikely scenarios such as dashboard rendering discrepancies or unexpected data corrections before the June 27 resolution check.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will a daily global surface air temperature anomaly of at least +1.5°C above pre-industrial be recorded between June 1 and June 26, 2026 (per Copernicus Climate Pulse)?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44075)
[Climate Pulse - Near real-time updates of global climate variables](https://pulse.climate.copernicus.eu/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, for at least one calendar day from June 1, 2026 through June 26, 2026 (inclusive), the daily global mean surface air temperature anomaly relative to the 1850-1900 pre-industrial baseline is at least +1.5°C, as derived from the Copernicus Climate Pulse dashboard (https://pulse.climate.copernicus.eu/). The pre-industrial anomaly for a day equals that day's global surface air temperature anomaly relative to the 1991-2020 baseline (shown on Climate Pulse) plus a fixed pre-industrial offset of +0.88°C (the 1991-2020 mean relative to 1850-1900). For example, a daily 1991-2020 anomaly of +0.63°C corresponds to +1.51°C above pre-industrial and resolves YES; if no day in the window reaches +1.5°C above pre-industrial, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: The check is performed on June 27, 2026. Only days within the June 1-26, 2026 window that have published data on Climate Pulse at that time are considered; if some days have not yet been published due to the dashboard's roughly 2-day update lag, the resolution uses whatever days in the window are available. If Climate Pulse is unavailable on the resolution date, the equivalent ERA5-based daily global 2m temperature series from Copernicus C3S (or reporting of the same data by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions)) may be used with the same +0.88°C offset; if no such daily data are available for any day in the window, the question resolves NO. The fixed +0.88°C pre-industrial offset is applied as stated regardless of any minor revisions C3S may publish.

factors.description: Multiple days (June 6, 7, 8) already show FINAL ERA5 data with anomalies of +1.53°C above pre-industrial, well above the +1.50°C threshold. The data source is the exact CSV underlying the Copernicus Climate Pulse dashboard.
Three days have margins of +0.026 to +0.033°C above threshold - large enough to survive minor data revisions, and the data is marked 'Final' status (not preliminary)

Small risk: the ERA5 CSV values could differ from what Climate Pulse actually displays on resolution day June 27, or there could be a technical dashboard outage, but fine print allows ERA5-based data as backup
Small risk: the +0.88°C offset could be applied differently by the question resolver, but the resolution criteria explicitly state this fixed offset

input.anchor: Based on the existing data: 4 days already exceed the threshold with finalized data, making this essentially a definitional/already-happened question. Prior ~97%.
input.assessment: This question has effectively already resolved YES based on existing data. The Copernicus Climate Pulse ERA5 daily data shows that June 6 (+0.651°C), June 7 (+0.653°C), and June 8 (+0.646°C) all had 1991-2020 anomalies that, when combined with the fixed +0.88°C pre-industrial offset, exceed +1.5°C. These are marked as 'Final' status data. The margins are +0.026 to +0.033°C above threshold - small in absolute terms but meaningful given that the data is finalized. The only remaining risks are: (1) the Climate Pulse dashboard displays different values than the underlying CSV on resolution day, (2) the question resolver interprets the criteria differently, or (3) some unforeseen data issue. All of these are very unlikely given the explicit resolution criteria and the fact that the fine print allows ERA5-based data as backup.
input.calibration_notes: This is not a predictive question about future events - it's effectively a definitional question about whether existing data satisfies the criteria. The data clearly does. The only discount from 99%+ is for unlikely data/interpretation issues.
input.key_uncertainties: Could the Climate Pulse dashboard show different values than the underlying CSV? Could there be a data revision even on 'Final' status data? These seem very unlikely.
input.tool_audit: research() successfully retrieved exact daily CSV data from ECMWF source file - extremely useful. fetch_url on Climate Pulse returned no data (JavaScript-rendered dashboard). ENSO research provided good context but isn't critical since data already shows exceedance.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was extremely effective at extracting the raw CSV data underlying the dashboard, which is exactly what was needed. The direct fetch of the Climate Pulse dashboard failed because it's JavaScript-rendered, but this didn't matter since we got the source data. For questions like this where data already exists, the main challenge is verifying the data matches what the resolution source will show.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence
All factual claims in the factors are well-grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1** claims June 6 (+0.651°C), June 7 (+0.653°C), and June 8 (+0.646°C) have Final ERA5 data exceeding the threshold. The trace shows the research sub-agent retrieved the exact CSV from `https://sites.ecmwf.int/data/climatepulse/data/series/era5_daily_series_2t_global.csv`, and the worldview research entry confirms these exact values with "Final" status. The factor says "anomalies of +1.53°C" — this is a rounded summary (actual values are +1.531, +1.533, +1.526), a minor imprecision but the core claim is correct.

- **Factor 2** claims margins of +0.026 to +0.033°C. Verified: June 6 margin = +0.031, June 7 = +0.033, June 8 = +0.026. The stated range is correct.

- **Factor 3** and **Factor 4** are speculative risk factors about dashboard discrepancy and offset interpretation. These are reasonable and appropriately weighted with low confidence.

### Resolution Criteria
Resolution criteria are present and clearly stated. The agent correctly identified the threshold: 1991-2020 anomaly ≥ +0.62°C (since 0.62 + 0.88 = 1.50). The agent used the exact data source referenced in the resolution criteria (the ERA5 CSV underlying Climate Pulse). The +0.88°C offset is applied correctly per the criteria.

### Pre-Publication Event (Case 1)
The question was published June 16, and the exceeding days (June 6-8) occurred before publication. However, the resolution criteria explicitly specify a window starting June 1, 2026. This is **Case 1** — the resolution criteria specify an explicit start date before published_at, so events within that window are legitimate.

### Double-Counting
Factors 1 and 2 draw on the same underlying ERA5 CSV data — Factor 1 establishes that days exceed the threshold, and Factor 2 emphasizes the margin size and data finality. This is mild double-counting, but the total effect (logit sum ~+4.6 before negatives) produces a probability around 99%, which is then tempered by negative factors 3 and 4, arriving at ~97%. The double-counting doesn't meaningfully distort the forecast given the overwhelming evidence.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All factor signs are correct: Factors 1-2 push toward YES (positive logit), Factors 3-4 push toward NO (negative logit).

### Contradictory Assessment
The narrative assessment is fully consistent with the factors — all point strongly to YES resolution.

### Counterargument Quality
The counterargument is reasonably strong. It identifies specific mechanisms that could cause failure: (1) CSV vs dashboard discrepancy, (2) ERA5 corrections bringing values below threshold, (3) possible column misidentification. It then appropriately notes these are very small risks given multiple independent days exceeding threshold and "Final" data status. This is adequate.

### Confidence Assessment
The agent reports 0.85 confidence in its 97% estimate. This seems calibrated — the agent has direct access to the resolution source data and the math is straightforward, but acknowledges residual uncertainty about dashboard rendering vs CSV values.

### Probability Assessment
The agent's 97% probability is well-supported. Three days with Final data exceed the threshold by margins of 0.026-0.033°C. The data comes from the exact source specified in the resolution criteria. The only realistic failure modes are dashboard rendering discrepancies or unexpected data corrections, both very unlikely for Final ERA5 data. I would independently estimate ~96-98%, consistent with the agent's 97%.

### Tool Audit
The agent says "fetch_url on Climate Pulse returned no data." Ground truth shows 2 fetch_url calls with 0 errors. The Climate Pulse fetch succeeded technically (0% error rate) but returned no useful data content because it's a JavaScript-rendered dashboard. This is a minor imprecision in description — "returned no useful data" would be more accurate than "returned no data" — but it's not a meaningful discrepancy.

### Worldview Consistency
The worldview research entry for Copernicus Climate Pulse data matches all values used in the factors. No contradicting worldview entries found. No prior forecasts on this specific question found in the forecast archive.
